data.title: Is DraftKings (DKNG) Starting To Look Attractive After Recent Share Price Pullback
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether DraftKings is still priced for excitement or starting to offer value, you are not alone. This article is written with that exact question in mind.
  • The stock closed at US$35.17, with recent returns showing a 3% decline over 7 days, a 1% decline over 30 days, a 1.4% decline year to date, an 11.7% decline over 1 year, and a very large gain over 3 years that sits alongside a 34.5% decline over 5 years.
  • Recent headlines around DraftKings have continued to focus on the broader US online sports betting and iGaming sector, including ongoing state level expansion and regulatory discussions that influence how quickly the industry can grow. These themes help frame why the stock has seen periods of strong enthusiasm as well as pullbacks when expectations are reassessed.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, DraftKings scores 3 out of 6. In this article we will look at what different valuation methods say about that score, then finish by highlighting a more complete way to think about what the current price really implies.

Find out why DraftKings's -11.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: DraftKings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today to arrive at an estimate of what the entire business could be worth right now.

For DraftKings, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flows in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $513.6 million. Simply Wall St uses analyst estimates where available and then extends those projections, with free cash flow in the ten year outlook rising to a projected $3.5b by 2035, with $2.5b indicated for 2030.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of US$87.80 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$35.17, this indicates an implied discount of 59.9%, which suggests the shares are currently priced well below this particular cash flow based estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests DraftKings is undervalued by 59.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 883 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for DraftKings.

Approach 2: DraftKings Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are not yet a steady guide, the P/S ratio is often more useful than P/E or P/B because it focuses on revenue, which usually stabilizes earlier in a business journey than profits or book value.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/S, while slower growth and higher uncertainty usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple. So what really matters is not the absolute P/S in isolation, but whether it lines up with the business profile and risk level.

DraftKings currently trades on a P/S of 3.21x. That sits above the Hospitality industry average P/S of 1.66x and also above the peer group average of 1.99x, which suggests the market is placing a higher value on each dollar of DraftKings revenue than on many peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for DraftKings is 3.26x. This is a proprietary estimate of what the P/S could reasonably be, after accounting for factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks, rather than just comparing with broad industry or peer averages.

With the current P/S of 3.21x sitting very close to the Fair Ratio of 3.26x, the shares appear to be roughly in line with what this framework suggests.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

data.description:
Comstock Resources, Inc.'s (NYSE:CRK) price-to-sales (or "P/S") ratio of 3.5x may not look like an appealing investment opportunity when you consider close to half the companies in the Oil and Gas industry in the United States have P/S ratios below 1.5x. What Does Comstock Resources' Recent Performance Look Like?

Comstock Resources certainly has been doing a good job lately as it's been growing revenue more than most other companies. It seems the market expects this form will continue into the future, hence the elevated P/S ratio. If not, then existing shareholders might be a little nervous about the viability of the share price.

If you'd like to see what analysts are forecasting going forward, you should check out our free report on Comstock Resources.

Do Revenue Forecasts Match The High P/S Ratio?

In order to justify its P/S ratio, Comstock Resources would need to produce impressive growth in excess of the industry.

Retrospectively, the last year delivered an exceptional 39% gain to the company's top line. However, this wasn't enough as the latest three year period has seen the company endure a nasty 46% drop in revenue in aggregate. So unfortunately, we have to acknowledge that the company has not done a great job of growing revenues over that time.

Looking ahead now, revenue is anticipated to climb by 20% during the coming year according to the seven analysts following the company. That's shaping up to be materially higher than the 2.8% growth forecast for the broader industry.

With this information, we can see why Comstock Resources is trading at such a high P/S compared to the industry. Apparently shareholders aren't keen to offload something that is potentially eyeing a more prosperous future.

What Does Comstock Resources' P/S Mean For Investors?

Generally, our preference is to limit the use of the price-to-sales ratio to establishing what the market thinks about the overall health of a company.

As we suspected, our examination of Comstock Resources' analyst forecasts revealed that its superior revenue outlook is contributing to its high P/S. At this stage investors feel the potential for a deterioration in revenues is quite remote, justifying the elevated P/S ratio. Unless the analysts have really missed the mark, these strong revenue forecasts should keep the share price buoyant.
